name: Gravel route through the mines and mountains of Asturias
name_original: Ruta gravel por las minas y montañas asturianas
description:
The mountains of Asturias preserve an almost virgin landscape full of unforgettable postcards that overflow with greenery. For centuries, coal has been extracted from its bowels, the industry of which has spread an enormous mining heritage and culture. In this Route we propose an adaptation to the gravel of the cycling ring of the central Asturian mountain in which we have included river swims and an approach to the Nalón mining basin.

The route starts from Mieres, where you can easily reach by road or train. It is a demanding route thanks to the abrupt orography of Asturias that offers heart-stopping climbs and views that take your breath away. The adventure takes place on beautiful dirt tracks and some stretches of lonely roads, so you can complete it with a gravel bike and enjoy the challenge and the landscape.

Both the distance and the gradient are impressive, but you can always divide the route into more stages with the komoot planner. The ideal time to cycle through the Principality is from mid-spring to mid-autumn, remember to choose a sunny day, although it is wise to bring a raincoat, the rain waters the greenery almost daily. Finally, remember to bring some spare parts and bring enough food and hydration, although you will surely find where you can stock up on local products in one of the small villages.
region_intro: Gravel bike trails around Mieres are situated in a mountainous region of Asturias, providing varied terrain for cycling. The area features significant elevation gains and a mix of surfaces, from paved sections to rough gravel and unpaved segments. Proximity to the Natural Park of Las Ubiñas-La Mesa and the Senda del Oso offers rugged and immersive experiences through wild landscapes and old mining tracks. These routes cater to riders seeking challenging climbs and scenic descents. Best gravel bike trails around Mieres The most popular gravel bike trail is Gravel route through the mines and mountains of Asturias, a 71.7 miles (115.4 km) trail that takes 10 hours to complete. This difficult route explores the region's mining heritage and mountainous landscapes. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Senda de La Baua – Campa Fresneo loop from Barredos, a difficult 41.6 miles (66.9 km) path. This loop offers extensive climbs and descents through the Asturian countryside. Local gravel bikers also love the Polio Lagoon – Rozamayor Recreation Area loop from Mieres del Camín, a 21.1 miles (34.0 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 50 minutes. Gravel biking around Mieres is defined by mountainous terrain, natural parks, and historic mining tracks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from challenging climbs to more accessible greenways. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 70 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Mieres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many gravel bike trails are available around Mieres?
A: There are 17 gravel bike trails around Mieres, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. The majority, 14 routes, are classified as difficult, with 3 moderate options.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on gravel routes near Mieres?
A: Gravel routes around Mieres are characterized by mountainous terrain with significant elevation gains. You'll encounter a mix of surfaces, from predominantly paved sections to rougher gravel and unpaved segments. Iconic routes like the Senda del Oso offer varied surfaces, including flat sections and more challenging gravel paths, often passing through tunnels, bridges, and gorges.

Q: Are there any family-friendly or easy gravel routes in Mieres?
A: While many routes around Mieres are challenging, featuring significant climbs and descents, the Senda del Oso offers sections that are notably flatter and more accessible, making them suitable for families or those seeking an easier ride. It's a greenway converted from an old mining train track, providing a smoother experience.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I might see on a gravel bike ride?
A: Many routes offer stunning views and pass through significant natural areas. You can explore the wild beauty of the Natural Park of Las Ubiñas-La Mesa or ride along the Senda del Oso, which showcases the natural and ethnographic richness of Asturias. For panoramic views, consider routes that pass near viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1074608" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Urbiés Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5151883" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coto Bello Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any loop gravel bike trails available?
A: Yes, there are several loop options for gravel biking around Mieres. A popular choice is the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31503318" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Senda de La Baua – Campa Fresneo loop from Barredos&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 41.6 miles (66.9 km) with extensive climbs and descents. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38155930" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Polio Lagoon – Rozamayor Recreation Area loop from Mieres del Camín&lt;/a&gt;, a 21.1-mile (34.0 km) route through varied terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for gravel biking in Mieres?
A: Mieres offers gravel biking opportunities throughout much of the year, but spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant weather conditions with milder temperatures. The mountainous terrain means weather can change, so it's always advisable to check local forecasts, especially for higher elevations.

Q: Are there cafes or pubs along the gravel bike trails?
A: Yes, particularly along the Senda del Oso, you can find amenities like cafes and picnic areas, making it convenient for breaks and refreshments during your ride. For other routes, it's a good idea to plan your stops or carry sufficient supplies, especially on longer or more remote trails.

Q: What are the typical distances and durations for gravel routes in Mieres?
A: Gravel routes around Mieres vary significantly in length and duration. You can find challenging long-distance rides like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1504801316/gravel-route-through-the-mines-and-mountains-of-asturias" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel route through the mines and mountains of Asturias&lt;/a&gt;, which is 71.7 miles (115.4 km) and can take around 10 hours. Shorter options, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38155930" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Polio Lagoon – Rozamayor Recreation Area loop from Mieres del Camín&lt;/a&gt;, are about 21.1 miles (34.0 km) and typically take under 4 hours.

Q: Can I access gravel trails in Mieres using public transport?
A: Mieres has public transport connections, and some routes, especially those starting or ending in towns, may be accessible. However, given the mountainous and often remote nature of many gravel trails, it's recommended to research specific route access points and public transport schedules in advance if you plan to rely on it.

Q: Are there any specific regulations for cycling in the Natural Park of Las Ubiñas-La Mesa?
A: When cycling within protected areas like the Natural Park of Las Ubiñas-La Mesa, it's important to respect local regulations. Generally, this means staying on marked trails, not disturbing wildlife, and practicing leave-no-trace principles. While specific permits for cycling are not typically required for general access, it's always wise to check the park's official guidelines for any updates or specific restrictions before your visit.

Q: What do other gravel bikers enjoy most about Mieres?
A: Gravel bikers often praise the diverse and challenging terrain, the stunning mountainous landscapes, and the unique experience of riding through historic mining areas and natural parks. The variety of surfaces and the rewarding views from climbs are frequently highlighted as key attractions.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the region's mining heritage?
A: Yes, the region around Mieres has a rich mining history, and several gravel routes incorporate this heritage. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1504801316/gravel-route-through-the-mines-and-mountains-of-asturias" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel route through the mines and mountains of Asturias&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, specifically designed to explore the region's mining past alongside its natural beauty. The Senda del Oso, an old mining train track, also offers a glimpse into this history.
tips: Nice compacted gravel track, very good surface and very well maintained. Ideal for gravel, hybrid and MTB bikes. The access from Felguera is concreted with a very steep slope.

Old copper mines that are being rehabilitated little by little. It has a very original and precious viewpoint.
The access track from Llamo is mostly made of concrete and with very hard slopes.
Suitable for Gravel, Hybrid and Mtb bikes.
Gravel track, with some stretch of land, from which there is a good view of the Texeo mine and the valley where Llamo and Rioseco are located.

An Asturian pre-Romanesque church, built in the middle of the 9th century. Unesco declared the Church of Santa Cristina de Lena a World Heritage Site in December 1985. The church can be visited from Tuesday to Sunday. It is closed on Mondays. The month of November is closed for holidays. The most comfortable pedestrian access is a road whose transit is restricted to wheeled vehicles, except for residents, property owners, and services.

Typical Asturian road stretch submerged in the greenery of the trees. On a sunny day with visibility you can appreciate the view of the surrounding hills, the meadows and their cows, the typical red-roofed villages and even some hórreo that have survived the passage of time.

Along this track that connects with Via Carisa, the views will leave you breathless. The altitude allows you to contemplate the landscape of the central Asturian mountain at its best. If you look down you see the intricate green valleys full of forests and alpine areas, always dotted with rural enclaves and meadows. If you look up you will see endless blue mountain ridges and, if you are lucky, you will also be able to see the huge exposed rock massifs of the Picos de Europa.

From this stretch, at almost 1,600 meters above sea level, the views of the Asturian landscape are almost unrepeatable. The panorama offers you green slopes and lines of bluish ridges that are lost on the horizon. In winter you can see some snowy peaks.
At this point the road runs like a balcony over the valley and Urbiés and all the surrounding farmhouses can be seen in the background. From this slope you can see the whole green pot below.
